Have only really played the multiplayer, not the campaigns.  The game runs well and is a timeless classic, with a strong community.  Ranked is a one of the less toxic I've encountered and overall a great time, but I mostly play with friends.  Sometimes I wish the devs would put more effort into improving QoL features such as keeping your multiplayer lobby together after a match, but maybe that's just part of the Y2K charm. The fact that this game is essentially almost 30 years old and still getting regular content updates tells you all you need to know.

It is a fun game and the bans (see below) did not appear for me for a while. The matchup phase is frequently bugged on mac. Instead of finding the match, the queue gets canceled - either with one of multiple error codes or by just never resolving. Obviously you are punished by a time out for this bug. I don't want to claim that I don't need the timeout, just that I don't deserve it and that I don't like it.  UPDATE: It got worse. (Probably only got worse for me, the punishment for the bug grows every time. The interval between the latest bug and the one before was the shortest though, so maybe it got worse for everyone.)  Sometimes, Instead of bugging in the queue phase, it bugs when loading the game. No way to restart without getting punished again. I would understand bugs in a new release per se, but why do you need to punish me for your mistakes...  EDIT: This seems to be a Microsoft issue, not a feral interactive issue, according to Microsoft support website they keep this system intentionally while knowing that it penalizes normal users: "We are aware of an issue where players may receive several error messages or difficulties while queuing for a multiplayer match. This then results in players receiving a notification that they've been penalized for disconnecting—despite not leaving the match intentionally. We're actively investigating this issue currently, and we appreciate your patience while we work toward a solution."

Like: - They sticked with origins of AoE II , without changing much the mechanics - Multiplayer and gameplay are improved and more smooth  Dislike: - I honestly don't like the visuals. Both menu and ingame they're not well polished, they tried to mimic a bit LoL and Fortinite style, I guess to attract more fanbase, but I rather prefer AoE II HD style by a mile, they could improve but still remain the same calm and clean design. - Multiplayer options and ELO are too clumsy and confusing, too many ELO types, ranked options. Let alone the UI , it's quite hard to understand in the matches if you will have good or bad teammates.  Final review: it is what it is, I would rather prefer AoE HD version 2.0, with same visuals, same simple ELO rating, with the newer mechanics and smoother gameplayer DE has. But it what we have, so buy it in a sale. I would never buy it a full price, it doesn't deserve the full price tag, it is far from being unplayable, but it's lazy done in comparison with HD edition.

Age of Empires III was good, but something was missing. Age of Empires IV was excellent, but too short. The first game has sadly become almost unplayable today.    But Age of Empires II… that was my first one, and to this day it still feels perfect.    The Age of Kings never truly ended. Since 1999, we’ve been conquering the world — the jungles of the Americas, the sands of Africa, and the forests of Europe. Campaigns featuring William Wallace, Joan of Arc, Saladin, and many others shaped not only my relationship with games, but also my love for history and real-time strategy games as a whole.    Age of Empires II is simply unique. Timeless, incredibly polished, and above all, endlessly fun to play.    Thanks to the constant stream of DLCs, the game now offers an enormous amount of campaigns that remain genuinely entertaining. Even my beloved ancient era has returned — Greeks and Romans alike. Childhood dreams of defending Thermopylae have finally become reality.    The dozens of civilizations now feel distinct thanks to the improvements and modernization brought by the Definitive Edition. The remake also introduced beautiful 4K visuals and updated models that still look fantastic even in 2026. Add the gorgeous classic soundtrack and you get literally dozens upon dozens of hours of entertainment.    Age of Empires II is simply a perfect strategy game — one that survived the test of time and still stands proudly at the top of the RTS genre.    History has never been this fun.    And thanks as well for the amazing DLCs that added not only the ancient world, but also Asia and even my own Czech nation complete with voice acting. It’s exactly the kind of love and care longtime fans hoped for.    Nearly 50 hours played… and I still can’t get enough. With so many campaigns left, there are still countless battles waiting for me.    Rating: 9/10

The immortal king of real-time strategy that puts on an absolute masterclass in post-launch support.  While other strategy games come and go, Age of Empires II: Definitive Edition stands tall as an unassailable masterpiece that has officially perfected the RTS genre. Forgotten Empires didn't just give the 1999 classic a beautiful 4K visual overhaul and a gorgeous remastered soundtrack; they completely rebuilt the engine to meet modern standards. The fluid unit pathfinding, modern queue systems, and brilliant automated farm reseeding instantly fixed decades of legacy jank without sacrificing an ounce of the strategic depth that made the game legendary. It is the rare remake that doesn't just ride on nostalgia—it sets a brand-new standard for how modern strategy games should feel to play.  The sheer volume of content available in this definitive package is completely staggering. With over 40 distinct civilizations, hundreds of hours of fully voiced historical single-player campaigns, and an incredibly robust co-op mode, it is an absolute paradise for solo players and history nerds alike. Balancing a massive economy of villagers chopping wood and mining gold while simultaneously micro-managing a dynamic army of knights, crossbowmen, and trebuchets is an incredibly intoxicating loop. Pulling off a perfect castle drop on an opponent's border or executing a brilliant flank with a group of unique units provides a rush of pure tactical satisfaction that no other game can replicate.  What truly cements this game's legendary status is its incredibly active, welcoming, and thriving community. Whether you want to play casually with friends against the brilliantly scaling AI, dive into the hyper-competitive ranked matchmaking ladder, or explore thousands of community-made maps and scenarios in the Steam Workshop, the game accommodates every skill level perfectly. The developers have shown a level of dedication that is almost unheard of in modern gaming, consistently delivering balance patches, performance updates, and massive expansions that introduce entirely new cultures and regions of history. It is a timeless, flawless "no-brainer" masterpiece that deserves a permanent spot in every PC gaming library.  10/10 — I built a giant wall, successfully converted a charging war elephant to my side, and lost four hours of my life in what felt like five minutes. Is a classic rts and has tons of campaigns for single player content. Base game has like 30+ campaigns and is worth it for the old AoK and AoC campaigns alone. Campaigns released after this version was released are also supposedly good but haven't gotten around to playing them due to sheer amount of content in this game. Multiplayer is also fun once you derank enough to your skill levels. Player numbers are high enough that you get games quite fast at your level. With return of rome you can also get aoe1 in aoe2.  Still has issues like pathing which can be annoying especially in campaigns with non-standard base layouts. Also starting multiplayer from average elo is a demoralizing experience (but they plan to change it in the future). But don't detract from overall enjoyment.  One of the best RTS ever and is worth to get if you are remotely interested in the genre.

I spent many happy afternoons with AoE2 during my youth, but this just isn't for me, anymore. I want to emphasize before I write this review that I am about as casual a player as it gets, and that is the heart of my problem with the game. If you bought this to play the slick new multiplayer mode, or if you consider yourself to be an "advanced" AoE2 player, you will likely be very VERY satisfied with your purchase.     If you're a casual player like me who just wants to play the campaigns and skirmishes against the AI, you may want to look elsewhere for your fun.    This new Definitive Edition looks and sounds absolutely gorgeous, but I find the hotkey controls to be incredibly complicated and clunky for a modern title, and facing off against an AI (or multiple AIs) that can individually control every unit and building simultaneously in an environment where longbows and swords can bring down castle walls without the need for siege engines, I find myself getting overwhelmed pretty often.    I feel like this game has been tuned very much for the players who are really good at it, which is a pity, because I don't ever remember struggling against the AI in the past. Maybe I've just lost a step now that I'm a middle-aged player with more to do with his days than play RTS games like I did in my youth.     There are a lot of people out there who will probably love this new version, and I wish them the best, but I just found my time with it to be stressful, and that's not what I want out of my gaming experiences these days.

Pure crap! What a rip off! I have hundreds (possibly thousands) of hours into this game on my XBox, and I absolutely LOVE this game!   I purchased the definitive addition and a bunch of add ons from steam, and when I played it, I spent most of the in game time with it running in the background while I tried reading through the guides and in the tutorial, learning the PC controls, since my experience was from the XBox with the XBox controller. Now that I'm 4 hours into the Steam version of the game, I found that a key feature is missing from the PC version, which I never realized just how necessary it is until I played this pile of crap, PC version, which doesn't have it.   The key feature is the Villager Priorities Menu. This feature allows you to add what percentages of your total number of Villagers are automatically assigned to which resource. Without it, the PC version requires a ridiculous amount of micro-managing of the Villagers, which makes it overwhelmingly unplayable for anyone that just wants to manage the resources at a high level, and not on a villager by villager level. This should at least be an enable/disable-able option for both versions.   I had no idea this feature was exclusive to the Console version, otherwise I would never have just dropped all this money on this game and most of the expansions. I requested a refund on the same day I purchased it, but since I was 4 hours in (again, most of that was just learning the control and feature difference between the console and PC versions), I was denied by Steam on getting a refund.   Besides this, there's one other refund I requested for a game I had 5 minutes worth of playtime in (which was all attributed to me trying to open up the game, but not successfully creating accounts with all of the additional launchers it had me install), which was also denied on the same day.   So now, on top of Steam's return policy being a complete joke, I have a game on both PC and Console with the same name and same exact edition (which allows for cross platform play, mind you), that do not have the same features/options. Both versions should have the same options/features, and not exclude features from one version or the other.   In 2026, consoles allow users to attach a mouse and keyboard, and PCs allow users to attach a controller. Why that is used as an excuse to limit features to either PC or Console versions only is just laziness! Massively disappointed!

Hesitant to give this a negative review, not because AoE II is a bad game or any glaring flaw, to the contrary, its one of the best RTS games of all time and this iteration on a technical level is flawless. Rather, Microsoft seems to add so much bloat in the newer DLCs to elaborate on existing motifs its now losing its identity. It started with the Dynasties of India DLC which had the Indian civilization split up. This was followed by The Three Kingdoms DLC which added the semi-mythical interpretation of the Warring states period that was somewhat before the scope of the game's time period (~500 AD-1500 AD). If it weren't enough that the thematic integrity was undermined, they added HERO UNITS to the multiplayer/ranked which has severely damaged the mechanical integrity of the game. As opposed to the separate data set we got with Return of Rome, where the civilizations are compartmentalized and balanced against each other. While this is in line with Microsoft making overtures towards countries that have a large presence in the RTS/AoE scene (the Koreans were rushed into The Conquerors expansion pack by Micorosft, see Sandy Peterson's comments on Twitter; Lac Viet was added to Return of Rome as a nod to the Vietnamese AoE esports scene) the Three Kingdoms DLC seemed to explicitly kowtow to Chinese demands with its poor implementation. With The Last Chieftains DLC, there's more gimmick units in an attempt to make these newer factions stand out. It's no doubt difficult with the sheer number of factions to maintain a coherent balance but the old design philosophy as initially established by Ensemble and faithfully carried through by the Forgotten Empires team in the HD edition seems to be eroded now with the churning out of more and more content.

[h1]Age of Empires II: Definitive Edition[/h1] [h3]Dishonest and Untrue to the Classic Version[/h3] [h3]Really Hard to Recommend[/h3]  Over the years, Age of Empires II has gone through so many changes, updates, and both good and bad DLCs that it now looks like an unrecognizable cultural crucible far removed from its original concept. At this point, it feels like an endless title that keeps expanding in such a disproportionate and careless way that the game no longer feels cohesive, but instead feels like an overloaded collection of unbalanced campaigns and civilizations.   The fact that there are so many balance changes immediately after new civilizations are released shows an incredible lack of proper planning and testing in the DLCs, and it makes it clear the game isn’t being expanded in a healthy way. The same thing happens with the new campaigns and historical battles, many of them are poorly designed, with objectives that don’t trigger properly, unbalanced missions, and campaign stories that are terribly built.  Another issue is the monthly and seasonal events system. It’s a strange mechanic that feels out of place in a strategy game, especially since it has little to no relevance in this title. I think that for many years now they’ve been pushing an awkward reward system to retain players and encourage them to come back after a certain period of time, but it feels completely unnecessary and forced for this type of game.  Overall, for me this game is still very enjoyable and nostalgic, and it has clearly evolved a lot over time. However, since most of these changes have been for the worse and the game doesn’t seem to be changing for a better direction anytime soon, I can’t really recommend this title, despite it being one of the games I grew up with and a classic among strategy games.  [h3]Gameplay[/h3] Age of Empires II has always been a very fun RTS since its release more than two decades ago. However, from the very start to this day most civilizations feel almost exactly the same, with only some minor changes for available technologies, unique units and bonuses that barely makes them feel different from one another. Of course, this is actually a good thing from a competitive standpoint, since it keeps the game balanced and ensures that matches depend more on player skill and strategy rather than on the selected civilization. But as a result, you end up with players relying on low-resource exploits like wall trapping and other cheesy strategies, which can feel frustrating and unfun to deal with, especially for casual players.  Another issue this game had since its early days was the lack of proper characterization for each civilization, since many of them shared the same architectural style and the same visual appearance for their shared units. Fortunately, part of this issue has been addressed through many updates that have finally made each civilization visually unique and more personalized after so many years.   Lastly, its gameplay is one of the most standardized in the RTS industry, which is both its biggest strength and its biggest weakness. On one hand, Age of Empires II has basic resource-gathering and unit management mechanics that are very easy to understand and learn, which is great for new players, but on the other hand, the lack of a more complex system makes the game very repetitive and dull over time.   [h3]Art and World Design[/h3] This version has nice graphics that feel refreshing and vibrant. However, this new visual style sometimes can also feel oversaturated. For example, the fog effect is very distracting and completely unnecessary for the game’s atmosphere, colors are excessively flashy, the texture blurring is extremely annoying and significantly reduces map visibility. Personally, I prefer playing without any of these options because they only make the visuals more distracting and reduce the overall match readability, which negatively affects gameplay.  The game also received a complete visual redesign for its main menu, cutscenes and the campaign selection screen, which now look much more polished compared to the original version. Personally, this is one of the aspects I liked the most about this version.  [h3]Music and Sound effects[/h3] The soundtrack is another aspect that received many improvements. The tracks now sound much cleaner due to their better audio quality and also more refined with the introduction of new short arrangements. As for the sound effects, there isn’t much to say. The game keeps the same audio cues and unit voice lines but with slightly improved sound quality.

Balance Is Gone — Abuse or Lose  Balance is no longer a thing, and it’s reached a point where there’s no turning back.  There are simply too many broken unique units. In some matches, you don’t even have a proper answer anymore — it feels like trying to fight rock with scissors or scissors with paper. Strategy barely exists.  The developers have also allowed countless cheesy strategies and don’t seem interested in fixing them. The only time you don’t feel handicapped is during mirror matches — and recent pro tournaments prove exactly that.  If you like a certain civilization and want to try something creative, you’ll most likely lose (or win) based on the map and civ matchup before the game even starts.  Players just abuse the meta: rush toward an unbeatable unique unit and mass it as fast as possible. Since maps are now easy to defend and the game is extremely fast-paced, this strategy almost always succeeds. If your opponent doesn’t have the counter civ, it’s basically a free win.  Unfortunately, this has happened to the entire Age of Empires franchise. The games are kept alive mainly by new DLCs and civilizations, each introducing even more broken mechanics. But in the end, it’s always the same:  Abuse the meta — or lose.

Here is a list of annoyances:  - The Mangonels lost their intelligence: they automatically fire in your own crowd and allies.  The original Age of Empires II The Conquerors Expansion fixed this bad problem back then.  - The Canon Tower has no effect against the Battling Ram, but the other canons easily destroy the Battling Ram.  - The Walls are not transparent. When I built walls next to trees there are always open spaces, but I cannot see where the open spaces stand, unlike in AoE 1 and 2 which had the transparency. Broken Walls in front of castles are not to be seen by the gamer. I just see Battering Rams going through my bricked defense lines. I cannot see the open space of a northly bricked defense line if a Castle stands in front of that.  - The unit may not move into the Transport Ship I ordered it to move into. But the unit automatically moves into a nearby transport ship next to the targeted Transport Ship. Even when the nearby Transport Ship is full, the unit still tries to move into the full Transport Ship instead of the targeted Transport Ship.  This is a constant bug: Units do not follow chosen movements: For instance Monks do not follow the Hero after being clicked doing that.  - The Transport Ships stack onto one another instead of being objects that block one another and cannot stick onto one another. The Transport Ships also block one another. They do both: stacking and blocking each other.  - The Computer Enemy Monks can convert my Walls and Gates. In the old Age of Empires 2 this wasn't possible.  - The Art of War Test games demand several goals at the same time: certain amount of food, certain amount of villagers and developing to the next age. Not separated. All at the same time. Ridiculous.  - The Hints in the Campaigns are often times stupid. Good example: 4. Orleans Campaign Hint Nr. 3 about Computer Green Chalons. It is false. Chalons is not vulnerable nor is it possible to make an example of them early as I tried. Chalons is constantly mass producing siege weapons and Knights and has his Villagers all in Town Center. All what this does is: Yellow sends Cavalliers and Long Bowers. Orange sends Pikemen and Knights. Constantly, permanently. It's a garbage "hint". For comparison, Burgundy (Purple) was vulnerable in the previous two campaigns and was always open for an early example.  The fourth game in the Barbarossa Campaign of the new Age of Empires is the worst yet.  4. The Lombard League, is not even a strategy game. It's like a Random Map with four Computer enemies against one player on a map where there is no island to hide and to exploit ressources, and the four Computer enemies just constantly storming. This is not "Strategy". One Castle and then four enemies storm you. Without an ally and no place to run, hide and rebuild.  No matter what STRATEGY I used, I couldn't do anything. I couldn't built castles after exploiting the stones because I didn't time get for it because of stormings. I have to built masses of houses for mass unit productions and no place for them to built where they are protected from the sea. I have no ressources to defend against four Comp. enemies at once, while I already defending against three enemies at once.  This fourth Barbarossa Campaign would be suitable for Post-Imperial Huns, but not Imperial Teutons. The Huns need no houses. The Teutons need masses of houses for their mass production. The Tarkan would be useful in the 4. and 6. Barbarossa Campaign. The Teutonic Knight was useless in the 4. and 6. Barbarossa Campaign.  The four Computer enemies play in mid game like the cheat Aegis is on with Post-Imperial Age units and technologies after a short time, while the gamer doesn't have any ressources for competition: moving Post Imperial is impossible, because you have no time for collecting the ressources. The four Comp. enemies have all the time. Four Comp. enemies who constantly storm you with all siege weapons. They constantly improve and have new powerful units which didn't exist in the original Age of Empires The Conquerors Expansion.  The Lombard League in Hard Mode is not playable with an infrastructure near the sea and building castles, because you get immediately destroyed by Computer Enemy Purple and Computer Enemy Orange once you built a castle. If you built a castle, you will be STORMED by the Computer Fake-Ally Orange shortly after. The "hints" are nonsense.  The enemies in the fourth Barbarossa campaign are everywhere. You cannot built walls without them attacking with high-powered range units. The worst are the laughable powerful Dromon Ships which didn't even exist in the original game. The kill any of my starter troops at ease with their 12 range.  It's impossible to built bricked structures until I can train an army because I'm being stormed with masses of War ships, Battling Rams, Onagers and Cavaliers. All at once, while I finished two bricked lines.  Venice doesn't control the sea with Galleys, Fire Ships and Dromons, but they destroy my entire infrastructure with them from the sea. There is no hiding from the ships on sea, except West. If I move anywhere but West, they will always reach me. West is near the starting fort. This absurdity was not the case in the old Age of Empires 2.  This is the main problem with The Lombard League:  1) I cannot flee with a transport ship to any place because of the bomber ships of Computer Enemy Purple. 2) I cannot exploit Stones and run and hide from the Computer Enemy and then built a miliary base, because the map makes it impossible: the Computer enemies are everywhere and can reach any place any time.  I just wasted alot of time with the fourth Barbarossa Campaign: Figuring a strategy out, but just waiting to be destroyed. This is absurd. 4 Computer enemies attack constantly with every siege weapon. Every siege weapon there is. Teutonic Knights and 5 Castles cannot defend from 4 assaults with all Siege weapons.  The only way to win this game in Hard is a Distraction: Move chased units into the castle of Orange, get the enemies to stand in front of the castle and built no castle. Otherwise impossible to win.  In the last Barbarossa Campaign, 6. The Sleeping Emperor, the Computer enemies Green and Blue seem to have a cheat: They seem to have infinitive ressources. Despite all the ressources long gone, Blue having no Villagers and both of them no Reliquies, both spam units nonstop. Blue spams soldiers and Siege Onagers, until his buildings are destroyed which he cannot rebuilt because he can't built Villagers despite having a Town Centre. Green never stops spamming his Elite Soldiers, his Cavallery Archers, his Arabestels, his Trebuchets and his Siege Onagers. Impossible to defeat Green.  For comparison: In the third game of the Joan of Arc Campaign, Fastolf's Army lost 1236 units and after that Falstof was finished, despite what seemed to me like infinitve ressources.  The worst game of the Joan of Arc campaign is the fourth. Early ambush against Computer Green without discovering the base disturbs and destroys the entire game for the gamer: he now faces four troops at once: two troops of Yellow, one troop of Green and three troops of Orange. All Computer enemies come at once with separate waves. What worked two times against Computer enemy Purple Burgundy doesn't work anymore, because all of his allies rush to his help. This makes the game uninteresting because early ambush doesn't work here anymore like it worked in the previous games against Purple of the Joan of Arc Campaign.

Gaming should be a relaxing experience. Not being able to run the game is, of course, not entertaining.  [b]The problem[/b] Once every few months, a major update comes out, adding new game contents. Sadly, each update has a chance to prevent the game from running on random computers.  [b]New libraries required[/b] The issue lies in new requirements in regard to dll libraries. Unfortunately the developer studio doesn't care to list these changes in the changelog, so users can only guess which dll files are required to run the game.  At first, the game started to require the library [i]xinput1_4.dll[/i], and this is [b]outrageous[/b] because one can simply rename the [i]xinput1_3.dll[/i] into the new one and the game works... So what's been really added to the game apart from a hidden requirement? What does the [i]xinput1_4.dll[/i] add over the [i]xinput1_3.dll[/i] if the game works by simply renaming the older version of the library into the new one? Nothing.  Then they started to require [i]api-ms-win-core-winrt-error-l1-1-0.dll[/i] and [i]api-ms-win-core-winrt-error-l1-1-1.dll[/i], and I'm going to skip the part about how I learned about this... Regardless, was it so hard to put it in the changelog?  [b]The current situation[/b] Last update broke the game, again, and some people are already complaining. This situation makes Age Of Empires II Definitive Edition too unstable for being considered entertaining, because now users would be supposed to learn by themselves which new dll are required to run the game, all of this because developers didn't spend two seconds to write down these changes in the changelog.  Finally, the game hasn't changed much, apart from new civilizations, since the beginning. So, what's the point of adding new core dll requirements? Why do Age Of Empires I & III Definitive Edition continue to work flawlessy?  Just buy an RTS that's more stable and whose developers don't plague their costumers with new dll requirements every now.

TL;DR: A legendary game turned into a disgraceful service. I love AoE2 — and I hate what it’s become.  [hr] [b]I honestly hate what this game has become — it's a disgrace to the gaming world.[/b] It's absolutely pointless to go into detail, just like it was pointless to report hundreds of smurfs for years, appeal about ridiculously broken matchmaking... a waste of time. I regret every wasted hour.  [hr] [b]First of all, the original game is an absolute gem.[/b] Easily in my TOP 3 of all time — no doubt about it. The devs and artists (even the DE ones!) are straight-up GODS.  [hr] [b]But overall? What a mess of a product.[/b]  For [b]32.99€[/b] (!!!) you get the [i]DEFINITIVE[/i] version — which means [b]YEARS[/b] of constant additions of new DLCs, civs, (dis)balancing, events, pathfinding changes, where every new version of it is [b]MEASURABLY[/b] worse. Every month there is a new meta strategy. Which will crush you, even if you dedicated thousands of hours.  Unless you're like me and fundamentally reject all DLCs, you could maybe buy some to watch everyone get your bought civilizations for free in six months.  [hr] [b]Matchmaking: Ridiculous rubbish.[/b]  I suppose it's made that way [b]ON PURPOSE[/b] to make players as angry as possible and keep them chained to a never-ending cycle of play for as long as possible. That's admittedly a fundamental goal of just about every game, and it's hard to actually blame the developers — but here it's just overdone.  [hr] Over the years I've met hundreds of smurfs (a smurf is a player who intentionally drops in ELO rankings so he can dominate the weaker ones) and reported on each and every one of them. No one ever cares, no one ever addresses the problem.  But on the other hand, the game does an excellent job of filtering out "ggs" as a vulgarism — you basically can't talk to the other person because everything is censored. Of course, people have found a way to send themselves to hell otherwise, but you get a chat ban for that.  You wanted to play Arena? No, you're going to play Nomad. You're gonna turn the game off so you don't have to? Then you won't play for half an hour. You want to play Mitchi? Then you'll have to wait three months. Imagine.  [hr] [b]Final thoughts:[/b]  If you want to play alone or campaign and don't mind the price tag, I can't say "don't buy it" — because it's the game of the century (last century). It's just that you're supporting a studio that can't implement reconnect or at least some waiting in [b]2025[/b], when [i]6 people[/i] are playing ranked match.  A studio that watches you get crushed by someone who has played [b]8000 ranked games[/b] but has a starting ELO like you. The destruction isn't close, it's absurd, hard, and actually funny.  [b]Nobody cares.[/b] And that's why it will happen to you [i]every night[/i].

You're tasked to gather 500 food while also training 21 villagers. a single villager costs 50 food, the food quota REVERTS as you spend it EVEN if you finished the objective! Great game, but the last three years of development convinced me to give a negative review, unfortunately. Simply because the last three years have been mostly spent on experimental DLCs that feel more like attempts at cash grabs or cutting down on development costs, one way or the other. Since Dynasties of India, the only normal DLC with civilizations and campaigns as we used to get back when is Mountain Royals, whereas the rest aren't.  Return of Rome had attempted to bring back AoE1 up to par with AoE2, but the devs again failed to develop its civs or (original) campaigns to the level of AoE2. And then they tried to bait in the Vietnamese competitive playerbase by adding Lac Viet (worked with Koreans in Conquerors, right?) and adding that new mode more in line with competitive scene's rules. This didn't work, of course.  So then came Mountain Royals with its price hike, and the Armenians being a messy faction by design. I'm not so much into balance, but I would've liked the civs to be grounded in history more than the older ones, which are the way they are due to grandfather clause more than anything.  Then Victors and Vanquished, which cut down on costs by a lot when they didn't have to make most of the scenarios. Just, take the existing free mods and resell them. Didn't even bother with any new civs at all on this one. This didn't work, and remains the worst DLC by review numbers. Many of them aren't even good scenarios, they're experimental pieces that just waste your time with slow gameplay. The most boring scenario in the game by far is Karlsefni and even William Wallace isn't as much of a bore.  Then there's Battle for Greece, which..... it's great, top even. Three problems, though: 1. it's not made by the main game's devs so it's not great to their credit, 2. it's not the main game mode so the main game remained untouched, and 3. it pretty much affirmed that AoE1 won't be explored any further..... Still, it's the best DLC we've probably gotten this entire period, definitely worth a recommendation.  And then there's Three Kingdoms which..... once again completely sidelined the core AoE2 content. Khitans and Jurchens are underdeveloped (or misdeveloped in case of Khitanguts), without voicelines or campaigns. And the core of the DLC? Three factions (not civs in any context that AoE2 has used until now) and three campaigns based on a historical fiction novel. It's a DLC that, much like Koreans and Lac Viet before it, tries to sell to a demographic by trying to appeal to them with civs. They didn't even base it on the Record of Three Kingdoms. They based it on a novel that all the pop culture creations based on Three Kingdoms use nowadays.  Oh, and I skipped the three portrait DLCs. Microtransactional, worthless garbage just to try and line the pockets a bit more.  The development has been bloating the game with failed experiments and cashgrab attempts, and the best DLC by a landslide is not even from the main dev team. If you can't make proper content, then what's even the point of having any development?

This is- sorry, WAS one of the best remasters out there. Flawless, and because of how dated AoE I is, instead of trying to develop that and this one, they migrated all the content into AoE II, which I originally didn't approve of, but now think was a pretty smart decision, this way they can work on both games within the engine and boundaries of just 1, on top of the added QoL from this engine into AoE 1. It's the 'Return of Rome' dlc if I recall, which only has mixed reviews because of some bugs, and AoE 1 fanatics malding that it's not a separate experience anymore, although being mad about having to pay extra for something you already had is reasonable, but that argument is made unreasonable because again, it's a glow-up not just a re-release, and on top of that they added a few extra campaigns and the Romans.  HOWEVER, AoE 2 in itself has slowly become a pandering mess. All the other games barring AoE 4 have been completely abandoned, although development is kind of slowing there too so I imagine it's not far behind. Their most recent dlc, 'The Three Kingdoms', more or less has zero place in AoE 2. It features entire mechanics that don't belong, AT ALL, and most of the added factions straight up play like their from an entirely different game, not a good sign. New and interesting, or innovating on something existing is all fine and well, but not when you add heroic units to an RTS game, give them to a specific subset of factions that are only dlc accessible, AND on top of that add those to official multiplayer and ladder/ranked. In other words, if a player plays with one of the complex Three Kingdoms and plays against somebody who doesn't, unless the person who doesn't is a god at the game they just die, because op heroic units.  Said they would add a DLC to AoE 3 and then said "Oops.. Sike!" So, if all of this recent history is anything to go off of, the entire Age of Empires franchise is in a precarious spot, and might already be doomed.. I'm genuinely scared of the future. Not necessarily because of AoE, but more of being scared for it. These games came out when I was a kid, and playing them as an adult feels absolutely amazing. But I can't overlook the recent and sharp decline in both quality, and effort, and can't help but think that this is the new trend, good games getting killed off one by one by the people that made them...

I have nearly 6,000 hours in this game. I've played for years, bought every piece of content, 100%'d single-player content, achieved top 150 on the ranked ladder, and participated in tournaments. This game was made for my brain. I've been trying to get old friends to play this game for years, and made plenty of new friends playing it. The core gameplay is timeless. If you are new, I can definitely recommend the base-game as an incredible deal for hundreds of hours of great content.  I'm here writing a negative review because the of the ongoing trend of out-of-touch, poorly made DLCs. It is also clear that the cross-platform adaptation to Xbox and Playstation have introduced numerous new bugs that have significantly worsened the gameplay experience and never fully been recovered from. We have been delivered disappointments for the past few years, with Rise of Rome and Victors & Vanquished, and now with the Three Kingdoms. It's hard to believe that this is the same team that gave us Dawn of the Dukes and Dynasties of India.  I, like many long-time and die-hard fans of the series, have been hoping for a Sinosphere DLC for years. It's an area that was ripe for expansion with lots of interesting and unique civilizations and empires. Instead, we got a half-baked mish-mash of a storybook campaign and two well-deserved civilizations that failed to properly encapsulate the diversity and interesting stories of the region.  This was the first DLC I forced myself not to buy, and it makes me wish I had been more strong-hearted in the past.  While the developers deliver community gold on a platter (properly balanced infantry, new Unique Unit, monk, and castle skins), they simultaneously are undermining the foundation of the game with poorly chosen civs and overly gimmicky new units. Some inspiration from other games in the franchise can be useful, but too much dilutes the strong foundation that this game was built and succeeded on.  It's a drop in the bucket, but I plan to leave this criticism up until I feel like the core issues with the direction of the game are addressed, and plan to no longer support the franchise financially. I encourage other long-time fans to do the same.

[h1]Age of Empires II: Definitive Edition[/h1]  By someone who survived the golden age of LAN battles and click-fueled empires.  There is something timeless about hearing a Town Center go up or watching villagers chop wood like history depends on it. Age of Empires II: Definitive Edition is more than a remaster. It is a polished time capsule.  But the moment you start a match, the nostalgia collides with reality. And the biggest issue is not balance or content. It is the interface.   [h2]The Return of a Legend[/h2]  Coming back to AoE2 feels like meeting an old friend who has aged well in some ways. There are new campaigns, new civilizations, smarter AI, modern matchmaking, production queues, auto-reseeding farms. These quality-of-life changes genuinely improve the experience.  Mechanically, the game remains one of the strongest RTS titles ever made. The strategic depth is intact. The pacing still demands attention and precision.  That part never lost its brilliance.   [h2]The UI Problem[/h2]  The HUD is where things fall apart.  Icons feel oversized and cluttered. Layout choices lack clarity. The minimap, which should be your tactical lifeline, often feels small and visually muddy.  The interface occupies too much space and offers limited customization. Even when minimized, it never feels sleek or modern. Tooltips are intrusive. Fonts feel dated. The overall presentation lacks refinement.  In a fast RTS where information is everything, readability matters. Here, it frequently gets in the way.   [h2]A Classic That Deserves Better[/h2]  It hurts to criticize it this way because the core design is still exceptional. But a clumsy interface disrupts flow, slows decision-making, and creates friction where there should be clarity.  Nostalgia can carry veteran players. New players will not be as forgiving.   [h2]Final Score: 7/10[/h2]  Age of Empires II: Definitive Edition still shines where it counts. Deep strategy. Tense matches. An iconic soundtrack.  But bringing back a classic is not just about preserving it. It is about refining it for modern standards. And in terms of UI design, this legend still feels stuck in the past.   [b]Pros:[/b] Faithful gameplay, massive content additions, strong quality-of-life upgrades, improved visuals   [b]Cons:[/b] Outdated and rigid UI, cluttered minimap, oversized HUD elements, weak visual ergonomics

Age of Empires II: Three Kingdoms – Where History is a Suggestion and Logic is Optional  If you’ve ever wanted to play a game where 3rd-century warlords out-tech the Renaissance and historical research is a myth, congratulations! The upcoming DLC is a dumpster fire of anachronisms, lazy design, and meme-worthy absurdity. Let’s break down this “masterpiece”.  1) Chu Ko Nu vs. Zhuge Liang: Watch the “grand strategist of the Three Kingdoms” get swiss-cheesed by his own rapid-fire crossbows. "The student has finally becomes the master!" A realistic depiction of ancient Chinese engineering! For context, China still has Chokonu with Shu as a potential adversary in skirmishes and multiplayer matches.  2) Lu Bu & Guan Yu: The One-Man Army Bros. Why build an army when these one-men can solo entire legions of Men-at-Arms? "Historical immersion" left the chat when the One-Man Army bros spin their halberds and send soldiers flying like they've been hit by some magical tornado.   3) Immortal Heroes: From Warlords to Super Saiyans? Cao Cao boosts attack speed like a medieval DJ. Liu Bei heals troops like a walking hospital. What if Sun Quan got sniped by a Cho Ko Nu? Just resurrect him like a COD killstreak.  These immortal heroes isn't just warlords-They are basically Super Saiyans with magical powers. At this point, why not let them summon a dragon?  4) 300 AD Tech Tree > Medieval Technologies: Who needs gunpowder when 300 AD War Chariots can shred apart 1500 AD Ottoman Bombard Cannons, Elite Janissaries and Portuguese Organ Guns in skirmishes? “Ottomans and Portuguese wish they’d invented wheels instead of guns.”  5) Fire Archers: Rocket Archers. Circa 300 AD These guys are launching rocket-propelled flaming arrows like it’s the Siege of Constantinople… except it’s literally 1,000 years earlier. Fun fact: The gap between the Mongol Empire founding and the founding of US, 580 years. The gap between Mongol Empire and Three Kingdom is 900 years. Shall we expect USA with Sherman Tanks in the next DLC?  6) Khitanguts: Civilization or Bad Fan Fiction? The devs mashed Khitans and Tanguts into a Franken-civ. Turns out, the Khitans were totally into Tangut engineering! Who knew? According to this DLC, the Khitan Liao Dynasty spent their free time ”appreciating” Tangut castle blueprints and mass-producing trademark camel siege units like a medieval knockoff flea market. In real-life, Khitans are known for their horse archers, not Tangut cosplay and Tanguts are famous for fortified mountain cities, not outsourcing engineers to their rivals.  7) Wei? Northern Wei? Same Difference! Why bother distinguishing between Three Kingdoms Wei 魏 and the Northern Wei 北魏 (a Xianbei dynasty 200 years later)? The Devs clearly googled or asked ChatGPT, got fed a hallucinated answer and got confused. For context, Three Kingdom Wei has Xianbei rider as their unique unit and a temple built by the Xianbei as their wonder.  8) Shu's Nanman Erasure: Forget actual allies with unparalleled potential for unique unit designs-Replace them with the generic 300 AD War Chariots, the perfect unit for Sichuan, Guizhou and Yunnan's mountains. Because mountainous terrain is totally ideal for wheeled vehicles! Next patch: Shu-Han's secret submarine unit.  9) “Chinese” Civilization Identity Crisis The base game’s Chinese civilization now awkwardly coexists with Shu, Wei, and Wu, all representing Han Chinese factions. This is like adding “Medieval France,” “Renaissance France,” and “Napoleonic France” as separate civs. Players are left wondering: Is “China” now a time-traveling multiverse? For context, the Three Kingdom period lasted for 60 years.  Final Verdict: This DLC isn’t a game—it’s a time-traveling dumpster fire where history is optional and logic is a casualty. Pandering to Dynasty Warrior fanboys and creating this defenestration (but with more villagers) is a poor decision. What's next for the patch? Scantily clad Diao Chan and Qiao sisters waifus, characters welding flaming swords or laser bows, or Zhuge Liang's magic feathered fan? Would still install to watch Lu Bu yeet a bombard cannon while screaming, "WHO NEEDS GUNPOWDER?!"

This new DLC is a massive deviation from the multiplayer experience and is not acceptable except by the most "imaginative" and anarchic design philosophy. At the very least, hero units need to not be permitted, even in a reduced variation, in ranked muiltiplayer.  I didn't like the addition of the Romans (we already have the Byzantines, we did not need another Roman faction), and I don't like the addition of Wu, Wei, and Shu to ranked play. They are outside the original timeframe of AoE2. The game is 25 years old and still popular, it does not need this sort of disruptive change. But as irritating as it is to those of us who want the timeframe to remain as it was, at least adding "civs" from antiquity doesn't fundamentally alter the multiplayer experience. Adding hero units does.  There are plenty of things this game has improved on compared to the CD version. The Definitive Edition is a good game by many measures. But the new DLC is changing the core of the multiplayer experience, and I don't like that.

I was really excited when I saw this came out. Age of Empires, but expanded with better graphics? Sign me tf up. But they changed the hotkeys which it's fine basically, just takes some getting used to after you've been playing this game how many decades? Not to sound like a boomer but considering it doesn't really feel like an improvement in any way seems a bit worse than pointless. Like hey we changed your interface. Cool, so it works faster and more smoothly right? Nah, not really. That'll be 30 bucks.  That's not my main complaint though; where this game really shines is the abysmal AI. How tf are you going to release a new game with AI worse than the old game? In games like this you always get some goofy unit behavior that you need to micromanage but this game doesn't even have basic pathfinding. Send your doods somewhere and watch them bumble into each other like the three stooges while you get raided. I've sent units somewhere and watched them get stuck on buildings or other units, just running in place because their pathfinding AI can't even handle that. Often AI enemies will get killed without even putting up a fight because they're trying to attack someone they can't reach, and they will try to pathfind through an entire army trying to get to one target way in the back. PVP might be a better experience, but I don't generally enjoy PVP, and that would still leave AI problems unaddressed. And considering this game is 6 years old, it kinda looks like unaddressed is exactly what it'll be.  IMO this game is a half assed reanimation of AoE2's corpse. I haven't even played this version because I find AoE2 HD to be more enjoyable. There might not be as much 'content' but at least you get can units to walk in a straight line. It's cheap and easy to update the graphics and tack on some more content slop so that's what they did, but under the hood it's actually regressed because writing competent AI is a bit harder and more expensive. AoE2, enshittification edition. Can't spare a dollar, but you can certainly charge one.

Before you go ahead and think I hate it: I don't. The new setting adds a lot of what I loved about the old AOE1 but in a new and modern coat that I like to see realized in full. Howeverm it also has a few serious issues: 1. Campaign:  - The campaign is very specific. Unlike in older installments, you don't get to choose your civilization. If you want to play trhe greek campaign, you have to slog your FULL way through 8 Persian campaign scenaria first. I ended up cheating through lots of them because they tend to be lenghty and demand a high difficulty level - Which brings us to campaign difficulty. It is VERY demanding, even on the easiest setting. The difficulty selector is merely beautification, you can choose somewhere between "start a few times over until you fully understand all the events and failed through the key points a bit to know what you are doing" and "punish yourself to the upper limits of professional e-gaming tournament level".  - Some scenarios are actively coded to be punishing. Enemies are many, they are strong, usually stronger than you, and you aren't told what to expect. "Face some bandits and defend convoys", only that they are hundreds and spread out all over the map so you end up being overwhelmed by how many convioys and bandits there are to handle fully simultaneously. In the beginning you can suddenly face tons of hoplites at a time you can only afford light units that hoplites eat for breakfast, and end up being ground down by the frequent and strong waves. Maybe you can hold yourself once or twicem but usually after sseveral try and error runs and possibly to the result of collapsing under the heavy weight anyway. - The scripts. It's heavily scripted. I like that because it adds to the feel of an actual campaign. However, the scripts are often not well explained, explained too late, or function in unexpected ways that often force you to start the scenario over.- If it's one you don't enjoy and just wanna get through somehow: Too bad. No campaign progress until you either made it (possibly spending DAYS to get to the part that intrigues you in the campaign) or start using cheat to skip those that slog you down.  - Timers. There's a LOT of them. Casual campaign play as we knew it, therefore, is d3ad.  In one scenario you get 3,5min to conquer 5 (!) "ambush points". then you're supposed to use these to ambush an enemy. Unfortunately, that enemy moves FAST. In the 3min you can maybe conquer 2-3 points. When you trigger them, they lagg a bit behind your command, ending up triggering AFTER the enemy passed through. Timers, heavy scripting and enemies being usually stronger than you do the rest.  2. Skirmish Okay, here I only have one gripe. The difficulty setting. You can choose "standard", in which the opponent builds a tiny village and rarely attacks with a handful of units. Everything below standard is just passive. Anything higher means the enemy COVERS the whole map in hundreds of houses, barracks and whatnot, actively taking the entire map to build all of New York or something. Attacks are more challenging, but still rather rare. Defensive structure is on the other hand still non-existing, even on higher levels. Overall it feels like you just choose the AI to do nothing, or just create one huge megacity with way too many buildings to take them all down and too many villagers to destroy AND prevent rebuilding at once.   On the plus side I love the new factions and their overworked unit models. Hoplites finally feel like hoplites, civic warriors with spear and shield rather than fully armoured late 16th century professional knights.  Overall I commend the attention to detail and the efforts to voice the campaigns in top notch quality, the animated and very immersive video sequences for each scenario. There's truly a lot of effort. But sadly, the campaign, my main reason to get this DLC, follows one, VERY strict line that only becomes enjoyable once you are highly experienced AND know the entire campaign and all of its little kinks and intentions. You get no freedoms anymore, you just have to know (at best in advance) and follow the intentions of the designer. That's why, for those who like to build cities, armies and take over other cities and armies, I could not recommend this one. Nearly every scenario has one of these "limited units, overwhelming enemy tactical puzzle with time pressure" parts. It feels more like a tactical RPG in which being outnumbered and outgunned is bread and butter. Casual players and AOE classic build-and-besiege enthusiasts are sadly apparently not part of the intended audience anymore.

Devs ruined pathing for melee units, making any ranged unit OP. Devs favor ranged units, making it hard to take them out even with counters. If you like cavalry and how they easily cut down archers and gunmen, forget it. In this game cavalry struggle to engage with archers and gunmen, archers and gunmen bounce away from them and cavalry barely hit them and then swarm about chaotically trying to reengage. Even heavy cavalry have no great advantage over archers and gunmen at close range in this game. It is necessary to completely surround the archers or gunmen to take them out in a good exchange.  But that brings us to cavalry archers, which are even more of a nightmare to deal with. While the devs make cavalry archers OP, they seem to hate horse cavalry, as they also made camel cavalry OP against horse cavalry. As soon as camel cavalry arrive on the  battlefield, your horse cavalry becomes completely useless.  Devs changed monks to make monks OP, so that even counters do not work.  Devs altered original gameplay with autofarm placement so that the game even more heavily favors economic booming over military focus, this also ruined certain hotkeys. Also, if you prefer to place the farms manually, when queuing multiple farms with the shift key the new feature makes it harder to place farms. This change puts the game clearly in the category of a hectic economic booming game than a true war game.  Devs penalize both military styles of gameplay, offensive and defensive. But especially military buildings are nerfed. Ranged units outside a castle are much more accurate and powerful, but when inside a castle they lose accuracy and power.  Devs also give some civs OP unique units (e.g. mamelukes, mangudai, unique archers, war wagons) that are effective against all other units, including siege, making it nearly impossible to push them as all they need to do is make one unit to counter a perfectly balanced army. If you like using rams against buildings, in this game it is very hard to do this in castle age against civs with OP unique units that are able to easily destroy rams and all other units from a safe distance!  Devs give some units pass through damage which means that a ball of these units is virtually invincible.  Devs introduce fun things into the game for variety, then nerf them later.  2026 now and devs keep making game worse: now they broke the shift command for queing villager tasks. And they added smaller hunt that can't be pushed, no doubt to frustrate military players who want a fast start even more.

I feel like it's important for me to state that this review is mostly meant for the online play, not the singleplayer. I'll have my thoughts on the singleplayer at the end.  So, after spending a good portion of time playing this game, I'll just say that the rose tinted glasses are coming off. I felt nostalgia playing this; and nothing more when I initially made the review for it, and close to a hundred hours of playtime (a good portion of it online) I can justly say, that this game is just...horrible. Not a single match you get into feels "fun", and the majority of players online are nothing but a bunch of sweaty crybabies who hate dealing with new people who wanna try and get interested in the online play, and do nothing but whine about how useless you are, or ragequit to leave you alone to fight the enemy team. There were very few people who were either helpful or chill, and actually gave me some pointers on how to get better (Quintem being the main man in that field); and honestly it was those people that even made it remotely tolerable. There's no room to goof around, no chance to have crazy sieges, no opportunities to enjoy any of the things that make civilizations feel unique; because in the endgame everything typically devolves into the same tactics: archer, mangonel, ram, or cavalry spam. And if you don't optimize yourself, then you're bound to get castle or guard tower rushed in the earlier stages of the game (especially when you're new). Overall, the majority of the online play of this game is just a cesspool of people that give LOL players a run for their money (some; if not most of them, probably are). The only times you'll actually get to remotely have any fun is when you play with friends; but that's also up in the air depending on if your friend decides to be a douche or not. There are tutorials and such, but they don't teach you ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ about how stuff works in online play, and the only way to learn is through certain mods, which is just wild.  As for the single player, it's good, solid fun for a bit, but it gets repetitive when you start understanding how the AI behaves. Not much else to say about it honestly, but can't say that I hate it (unlike the online play)   Overall though: Multiplayer: 2/10 Singleplayer: 8/10

Spent an hour being gaslit by my friends yelling "keybinds keybinds keybinds" as the operative, standard way of playing this game.    AoE is, probably, by definitions an RTS. It does NOT lend itself well to army battling. Units are individual, not marked for what their type is (ranged, melee, siege, cavalry). If you're my buddies Nate or Al, this is the entirety of the BEST part about AoE. If you've played any other, like literally any other RTS, keybindings do not make a game.     On top of that, this whole "Fast Castle Technique" is not a "technique", it's literally just how you play the game. It's not a skill, it's not a strategy, it's certainly not a technique, you literally are just rushing to castle so you can either send a bunch of bowmen, or place towers, near the enemy base and declare yourself "good at RTS".     There's no structure to how you manage your units. The ♥♥♥♥♥♥, stupid, useless little "+" hidden in the bottom left hand corner is NOT an end-all-be-all solution, and is one of the worst mechanical systems to "managing" your army I've seen in any game.    You are required to find and obtain esoteric lore from one of the 2 "pros" who play this game, in which their "beginner's guides" sound like someone is firing a fully automatic machine gun and telling you "just do this" as they smash their keyboard with (and you'll get a bonus point for guessing what the term I'm about to use here is) K E Y B I N D I N G S, because that's the only way to make this abortion of an excel sheet game "playable".    Completely unaware as to how this game has a cult following (it's a 90's game, so it's actually pretty easy, since this is one of 4 games released at the time) and why you nerds spend so much time stan'ing it.     Infinitely better RTS games that you could play with actual UI and mechanics, and not just having to default to learning how to use keybindings to now "be good" at the game.

My experience of multiplayer includes a frustrating amount of time (50% or more) trying to find a fun competitive game (so much so i watch/play other things while I queue that's how long the process is). Match making is poor (regularly matching very uneven elos) and riddled with disconnects (either due to players quitting or the game crashing) which there is a chance you will then be punished with a ban for... when you eventually find a game that is fair on a map you actually want to play (currently players are forced to play a variety of maps they do not like while many people disconnect when they get a map they dislike) the game is fun, if you look past the poor pathing (hello regroup logic and stuck villagers). I imagine it is horrendous for new players as they also have to contend with the games steep learning curve and although 1v1 may have a friendlier player base (but also very predictable and eventually boring play style), team games are riddled with toxic unpleasant behaviour and you see the same names doing the same things again and again. I abandoned single player a long time ago as on at least 4 occasions all progress I had made reset its self leaving me having to play through everything from the beginning... it is a shame to see a game that can be very fun, continuously feel like it is not worth the time/effort required to enjoy it for such little return

first of all definitive edition is ♥♥♥♥. microsoft the indie company didn't have enough budget. you get issues with 2 screens, fps drops and much more.    in the first game it was all about catapults and ballista in here there is rock paper scissors mechanics as there was in age of mythology. can't say I enjoy that but I don't play multi anyways so it doesn't bother me.     the second big change is population increase. the battles are way more chaotic and since I am not a big fan of min maxing anyways I don't really know what to think about it. I wanted to play this game on hardest diff and got to the half of europe screen on that. but then I realized that this game was too long and some campaigns are flat out ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. I never defeat an enemy on my first try no matter what I do. but if I watch a video where a guy plays it online like robocop with perfect gameplay I see which enemies use what and what I actually need to do in the mission and then I can do the mission way more easily. there is this level where the enemy is unstoppable and I can't beat him. I go back saves and I am thinking that I am playing on easy diff what is happening? it seems the enemy attacks with his big force when a certain time passes OR if you go to the next age... just tell us this man. it is as if 5 different directors made the game without coming to a consensus.    so yeah as single player this game is a really bad experience at this day and age. I would like to comment on its time but I was playing mythology more than this so I can't.     you should leave the experience in your memories so they will still be sweet huh.     if you will still play this, keep in mind that I turned diff to easy when I got to around 20 percent of the game and even then it took 200 hours to finish with absolutely no dlc. I, personally do not recommend Age of Empires II.  Even on "easy" settings, The A.I. relentlessly attacks; not giving you the time needed to build up your economy so you can build up your military.  If your a beginner, I don't think you'll find this enjoyable.  Update:  Anyone that finds my comment amusing can laugh all they want.  You've probably been playing this game for years & consider yourselves "advanced players," which is great!  But, for beginners & folks that either want to take a little more time & learn how to play and develop their own strategy or perhaps just want a more relaxed/balanced gaming experience, this is not the game for them.   I would recommend Age of Empires III or IV for a better balanced play.
